serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Red Bull can of 250 mL, which is the typical single-serve unit sold in Singapore and internationally.

nutrient_source.title: Red Bull Energy Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Red Bull 红牛 (cr_id G00010957). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glucose drinks and sugar-sweetened beverages — GI values for sucrose/glucose blend beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Red Bull in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 68 based on the closest analogous entry: sucrose-glucose blended sugar-sweetened carbonated beverage (GI ~65–72 range per University of Sydney GI Database). Red Bull contains sucrose and glucose as primary carbohydrate sources (10.2g/100g, all sugar, zero fibre), consistent with a medium-GI beverage. GL per 250 mL serving = (68 × 25.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 17.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Caffeine-induced acute blood pressure elevation combined with high sugar content (10.2g/100g) exceeding the 5g/100g beverage sugar threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Red Bull entirely; caffeine (~32 mg/100 mL) causes acute blood pressure spikes, and the high sugar load (10.2g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, both of which are contraindicated in hypertension management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (10.2g/100g, all rapidly absorbed sucrose and glucose) with zero dietary fibre, causing immediate postprandial blood glucose spikes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Red Bull; the 10.2g/100g sugar load delivered in liquid form with zero fibre causes rapid blood glucose elevation, and regular caffeine consumption may further impair insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Caffeine content causing acute cardiovascular stress, combined with high sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Red Bull; caffeine in energy drinks is associated with acute increases in heart rate and blood pressure, while the high sugar content (10.2g/100g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (10.2g/100g) with zero fibre promotes systemic inflammation; no anti-inflammatory factors present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular Red Bull consumption; the high sugar load promotes pro-inflammatory pathways, caffeine may worsen RA-related fatigue and interact with RA medications, and the drink provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Bull Energy Drink (红牛功能饮料) is a globally recognised ultra-processed carbonated sugar-sweetened energy drink originally developed in Austria and widely consumed in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is sold in a standard 250 mL aluminium can and is ready to drink without preparation. The drink has a sweet, slightly tart, citrus-adjacent flavour with light carbonation. Its primary active ingredients are sucrose, glucose, taurine (~400 mg/can), caffeine (~80 mg/can), and B-vitamins (B3, B5, B6, B12). Per 100 mL, it provides 43 kcal, 0.46g protein, 0.0g fat, and 10.2g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with 39 mg sodium and negligible potassium (3 mg). Dietary fibre is absent. Clinically, Red Bull is a high-concern beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its rapidly absorbed sucrose-glucose blend causing immediate blood glucose spikes. It is equally high-concern for hypertension due to caffeine-induced acute blood pressure elevation and sugar-driven sodium retention. For cardiovascular disease, the combination of caffeine stress and high sugar intake contributing to dyslipidaemia makes it inadvisable. The drink is ultra-processed, contains no meaningful micronutrient density beyond added B-vitamins, and should be avoided by individuals managing any cardiometabolic condition. It is commonly consumed as a stimulant, study aid, or mixer in social settings.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Red Bull?
Is Red Bull bad for blood pressure?
Is Red Bull suitable for hypertension?
Can I drink Red Bull if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id G00010957) with confidence 0.95. The food is an internationally standardised commercial product with a fixed formulation, supporting high confidence in the per-100g values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sucrose-glucose blended beverages, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation — Red Bull contains no fat by design as a carbonated beverage; this is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — this is a liquid sugar-sweetened beverage with no fibre-containing ingredients. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero by product formulation. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo database for this beverage product. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product; while Red Bull contains added B-vitamins (B3, B5, B6, B12) per product label, exact per-100g values are not available in the HPB approved source. Input food_group was incorrectly listed as 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients'; corrected to 'Beverages / Sugar-sweetened energy drinks' based on product identity. Input record_type was 'dish'; this is a packaged commercial beverage product. Occasion tags 'lunch' and 'dinner' from input cultural data are not appropriate for an energy drink and have been replaced with contextually accurate meal_context values. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-glucose blended sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct Red Bull GI entry exists in the approved database.
